hook: You already know how to compute proportions from sample data — for example, if 55 out of 100 surveyed voters support a candidate, the sample proportion is 0.55. But here's the gap: how do you decide whether that 0.55 is genuinely different from the claimed 50%, or whether it could just be random chance? You need a formal procedure that weighs the size of the discrepancy against the natural variability of sampling. Can you determine, with statistical rigor, whether the evidence is strong enough to reject a claim about a population proportion?
concept: A one-sample z-test for a proportion lets you decide whether a sample proportion is statistically different from a hypothesized population proportion $p_0$. Formally, under $H_0: p = p_0$, the test statistic is $z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}$, which follows a standard normal distribution when the conditions (randomness, independence, and at least 10 expected successes and failures) are met.
anatomy: The test has several named components: $\hat{p}$ is the sample proportion (successes divided by $n$); $p_0$ is the null-hypothesis proportion, the specific value being tested; $n$ is the sample size; the denominator $\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}$ is the standard error of $\hat{p}$ under $H_0$, measuring how much $\hat{p}$ naturally varies; the resulting $z$-score measures how many standard errors $\hat{p}$ sits from $p_0$; and the $p$-value is the probability of observing a $z$-score at least as extreme as the one computed, used to decide whether to reject $H_0$.

worked_example.description_latex: We begin by stating the hypotheses. A manufacturer claims that 40% of customers prefer their new product, but a consumer group suspects the true proportion is higher, so the null hypothesis sets $p_0 = 0.40$ and the alternative is one-sided ($p > 0.40$). Identifying the direction of $H_a$ is essential because it determines whether we use a one-tailed or two-tailed $p$-value.

Next we record the sample information and compute the sample proportion $\hat{p}$. Dividing the number of successes (72) by the sample size (150) gives $\hat{p} = 0.48$, which is the observed proportion we will compare to $p_0 = 0.40$.

Before computing anything, we must verify the large-counts condition using $p_0$ (not $\hat{p}$), because under the null hypothesis it is $p_0$ that governs the distribution. Both 60 and 90 exceed 10, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal and the z-test is valid.

Now we compute the test statistic by plugging into the formula. The standard error is $\sqrt{0.40 \times 0.60 / 150} = \sqrt{0.0016} = 0.04$, so the $z$-score is $0.08/0.04 = 2.00$, meaning $\hat{p}$ is exactly 2 standard errors above the null value.

Because $H_a$ is one-sided (greater than), the $p$-value is the area to the right of $z = 2.00$ in the standard normal distribution, which is approximately 0.0228. Since $0.0228 < 0.05$ (the significance level $\alpha$), we reject $H_0$ and conclude there is statistically significant evidence that the true proportion exceeds 40%.

partial_example.description_latex: A university claims 25% of its students commute from off-campus housing. A researcher surveys 200 students and finds 60 commuters. We state a two-sided alternative because we're checking whether the true proportion differs in either direction from 0.25.

We compute the sample proportion: 60 commuters out of 200 students gives $\hat{p} = 0.30$. We have also verified the large-counts condition: $np_0 = 200(0.25) = 50 \geq 10$ and $n(1-p_0) = 150 \geq 10$.
Now compute the test statistic. Evaluate the standard error $\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}$ and then divide the difference $\hat{p} - p_0$ by it to find $z$. What numerical value do you get?
Using the $z$-score you just found and the fact that $H_a$ is two-sided, compute the $p$-value as $2 \times P(Z > |z|)$ and state whether you reject or fail to reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A coin is flipped 100 times and lands heads 52 times. Test $H_0: p = 0.50$ vs.\ $H_a: p \neq 0.50$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. Compute the $z$-test statistic.
A survey of 400 adults finds that 220 support a new policy. Test $H_0: p = 0.50$ vs.\ $H_a: p > 0.50$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. State the $z$-statistic and conclusion.
A quality control inspector checks 250 items and finds 30 defective. The company claims the defect rate is 10%. Test $H_0: p = 0.10$ vs.\ $H_a: p \neq 0.10$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

A researcher wants to detect a departure from $p_0 = 0.30$ using a one-sided test ($H_a: p < 0.30$) at $\alpha = 0.01$. A sample of 500 yields $\hat{p} = 0.26$. Compute the $z$-statistic, find the exact $p$-value (use the fact that $P(Z < -1.95) \approx 0.026$ and $P(Z < -2.50) \approx 0.006$), and state your conclusion.

A clinical trial tests whether a new drug cures a condition more often than the current 35\% cure rate. Of 180 patients given the new drug, 81 are cured. (a) State hypotheses, (b) verify conditions, (c) compute $z$, (d) find the $p$-value, and (e) interpret in context at $\alpha = 0.05$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \hat{p} = 81/180 = 0.45;\; z = \frac{0.45-0.35}{\sqrt{0.35(0.65)/180}} \approx \frac{0.10}{0.03559} \approx 2.81;\; p\text{-value} = P(Z>2.81) \approx 0.0025 < 0.05;\; \text{Reject } H_0\text{: significant evidence the new drug has a higher cure rate}

common_mistakes: Using $\hat{p}$ instead of $p_0$ in the standard error formula. The standard error under the null hypothesis must use $p_0$ because we are computing how variable $\hat{p}$ would be if $H_0$ were true; using $\hat{p}$ gives an incorrect denominator and a wrong $z$-score. Always plug $p_0$ into $\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}$.

Forgetting to double the tail probability for a two-sided test. When $H_a: p \neq p_0$, the $p$-value is $2 \times P(Z > |z|)$ because extreme values in either direction count as evidence against $H_0$; taking only one tail cuts the $p$-value in half and can lead to incorrectly rejecting $H_0$. Always check whether your alternative is one-sided or two-sided before computing the $p$-value.

Checking the large-counts condition with $\hat{p}$ instead of $p_0$. The condition $np_0 \geq 10$ and $n(1-p_0) \geq 10$ ensures the null sampling distribution is approximately normal; using $\hat{p}$ checks the wrong distribution and can give a false green light when the test is actually invalid. Always use the null value $p_0$ when verifying this condition.
untested_variants: Determining the minimum sample size $n$ needed to achieve a specified power (probability of correctly rejecting $H_0$) for a given effect size $p_a - p_0$.
Constructing and interpreting a confidence interval for $p$ and connecting it to a two-sided hypothesis test conclusion (the duality between confidence intervals and hypothesis tests).
Performing the test when data are given as a two-way table or in percentage form rather than as raw counts, requiring the student to extract $n$ and $\hat{p}$ before applying the formula.
Comparing the z-test conclusion to a chi-square goodness-of-fit test for a single proportion, noting that $z^2 = \chi^2$ and reconciling the two approaches.
